intro: Prayer is direct address to God — "the intercourse of the soul with God" — encompassing praise, thanksgiving, confession, intercession, and petition. Throughout Scripture it is assumed that the living God hears and responds to prayer. The Psalter provides the richest biblical model of prayer in all its dimensions. Hannah's silent, intense supplication (1 Sam. 1:13–15) was at first mistaken for drunkenness. Daniel prayed three times daily toward Jerusalem even under threat of death (Dan. 6:10). Jesus taught his disciples to pray with the Lord's Prayer as a model (Matt. 6:9–13; Luke 11:2–4), emphasized persistent prayer (Luke 18:1–8), and himself prayed at critical moments — at his baptism (Luke 3:21), in Gethsemane (Matt. 26:36–46), and from the cross. Paul commands unceasing prayer (1 Thess. 5:17) and connects it with thanksgiving and the peace of God (Phil. 4:6–7).
